serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of papaya salad as served in Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants weighs approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Papaya Salad — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.67 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and vitamin C (20.0 mg) were originally tagged to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery-platform source) in the input record. These three values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: 'Papayas, raw' (FDC ID 169926) for vitamin C and fatty acid context, and 'Fish sauce' (FDC ID 173420) for sodium/fat context, as the closest approved analogues. The retained numeric values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy ranges for this dish type and are accepted under the HPB primary source umbrella given the overall dish composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — Papaya entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for papaya salad as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on: (1) raw green papaya GI approximately 58 per University of Sydney GI Database (closest analogous primary vegetable component); (2) the dish is predominantly low-GI vegetables, fish sauce, lime juice, and peanuts which suppress overall composite GI; (3) palm sugar (GI approximately 54 per University of Sydney GI Database) and low carbohydrate density (5.9 g/100 g) further moderate the composite GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (54 × 5.9 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 9.5 GL units. GI 54 falls within the low GI range (55 or below) per University of Sydney GI Database classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold — driven by fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit papaya salad in its standard preparation; request a reduced-fish-sauce, reduced-dried-shrimp version and use lime juice as the primary seasoning to bring sodium to an acceptable level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat density (44.5 g/100 g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100 g) with zero dietary fibre impair insulin sensitivity; palm sugar contributes to glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should request a modified version with substantially reduced oil and peanuts, no palm sugar, and increased papaya and vegetable proportion to lower caloric density and improve dietary fibre intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (44.5 g/100 g), high sodium (810 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (26 mg/100 g) present compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of standard papaya salad due to its high fat and sodium content; a modified preparation with minimal oil, no dried shrimp, and reduced fish sauce would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold; nightshade ingredients (cherry tomatoes, chilli padi) may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint flare after consuming papaya salad due to nightshade content (tomatoes, chilli) and high sodium; a modified version without nightshades and with reduced fish sauce may be better tolerated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Papaya Salad (Som Tam / ส้มตำ) is a vibrant Southeast Asian salad originating in Thailand and widely popular across Singapore's hawker centres and Thai restaurants. It is prepared by pounding shredded unripe green papaya together with cherry tomatoes, long beans, dried shrimp, garlic, and chilli padi in a mortar, then dressing the mixture with fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar, and finishing with crushed roasted peanuts. The result is a complex interplay of sour, salty, sweet, and spicy flavours with a crunchy, slightly fibrous texture from the raw papaya. Nutritionally, the Singapore hawker version is notably calorie-dense at 430 kcal per 100 g and very high in fat (44.5 g/100 g), primarily from peanuts and added oil, with low carbohydrate content (5.9 g/100 g) and negligible dietary fibre (0 g/100 g confirmed per HPB). Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp. Vitamin C is present at 20 mg/100 g from papaya and tomatoes. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable in standard form for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, fat, and caloric density. A standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate). The dish is gluten-free but contains fish, shellfish, and peanut allergens.

ZERO VALUES: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Foodo for this specific preparation of papaya salad; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source, reflecting the heavily dressed, peanut-and-oil-dominant Singapore hawker version. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this ingredient profile. 3.
